EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and slow moving low pressure system across the north central Atlantic generated large swells which began affecting Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands late in the day on Saturday December 26th, 2009. Several different batches of swell were forecast to affect the local islands, with the largest anticipated for late in the day Monday December 28th through Tuesday December 29th, 2009.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf affected Saint Croix causing significant beach erosion and some minor coastal flooding on the island's east end and north shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper level low was located just north of the local islands. Late in the morning an area of thunderstorms moved over Saint Croix and produced very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, thunder, and gusty winds, were reported over Fredericksted, Saint Croix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong area of pre frontal convergence over the Virgin Islands produced active weather conditions. Heavy rains affected Saint Thomas which prompted the government to close offices and schools at midday. Flooding was reported at Hess Road, Williams Delight, and Mon Bijou on Saint Croix; Brookman Road, Havensight, Sub Base, and Adelita Cancryn School and Banco Popular on Saint Thomas; and Centerline Road on Saint John. Flooding was also reported at the King Airport main terminal building on Saint Thomas. Unofficial reports from Fortuna Bay, on the west end of Saint Thomas indicated rainfall amounts of around 8 inches overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent periods of heavy rain affected the Saint Thomas airport during the night and morning hours. Urban and road flooding was reported on several parts of the island. The ASOS rain gauge recorded 3.57 inches for the 24 hour period ending at midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong area of pre frontal convergence over the Virgin Islands produced active weather conditions. Heavy rains affected Saint Thomas which prompted the government to close offices and schools at midday. Flooding was reported at Hess Road, Williams Delight, and Mon Bijou on Saint Croix; Brookman Road, Havensight, Sub Base, and Adelita Cancryn School and Banco Popular on Saint Thomas; and Centerline Road on Saint John. Flooding was also reported at the King Airport main terminal building on Saint Thomas. Unofficial reports from Fortuna Bay, on the west end of Saint Thomas indicated rainfall amounts of around 8 inches overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain affected the Saint Croix airport during the day. Urban and road flooding was reported in some sectors of the island. An NWS rainfall spotter close to Fredericksted recorded 2.45 inches for the 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave emerged from the coast of Africa on August 25th, which moved westward near the Cape Verde Islands on the 26th.  The system organized as it crossed the Atlantic, developing a low pressure area by the 28th. The system became well-enough organized to be classified a tropical storm during the afternoon of September 1st.  A tropical storm watch was issued for the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico at 5 pm September 2nd, followed by a tropical storm warning at 11 am on September 3rd. The system moved generally westward, and experienced moderate vertical wind shear through most of its existence as a tropical cyclone.  As the surface circulation left most of the associated thunderstorms behind, the system tracked south of due west due to the low-level flow pattern Erika was embedded within. By the 3rd of September, Erika weakened into a tropical depression at 5 pm on September 3rd, and soon devolved into a remnant low during the evening.  The remnant disturbance led to heavy rains over the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico,  which caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 1.61 inches for the 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface trough moving across the area in combination with upper level low and associated trough north of Puerto Rico, produced numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms across the U.S. Virgin Islands. The heavy rains Monday caused flooding problems across St. Thomas and St. John. Saint Thomas Airport rain sensor recorded 1.92 inches for the 24 hour period ending at midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The bridge across the road near the Westin Resort and Villas on St. John was closed. Localized flooding was also reported near the Marketplace shopping center and in Cruz Bay. There were also rock slides on Bordeaux Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface trough moving across the area in combination with upper level low and associated trough north of Puerto Rico, produced numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms across the U.S. Virgin Islands. The heavy rains Monday caused flooding problems across St. Thomas and St. John. Saint Thomas Airport rain sensor recorded 1.92 inches for the 24 hour period ending at midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mandela Circle in the Havensight area of St. Thomas was reported flooded. The area around Mandela Circle also flooded Monday afternoon. Vitraco Mall area was reported flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms with heavy rain and gusty winds affected the island of Saint Thomas during the early morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint Thomas airport ASOS indicated heavy rain for a period of one hour and a half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Saharan dust (SAL) covered the U.S. Virgin Islands for several days causing hazy skies and reduced visibilities.  The Saint Croix airport ASOS sensor indicated occasional periods of horizontal visibilities varying between 6 and 9 miles during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities between 6-9 miles were reported at the Saint Croix airport due to saharan dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well organized tropical wave with an area of low pressure and significant thunderstorm activity moved offshore the African coastline on August 9th.  The system moved northwest until reaching the southern Cape Verde Islands, then turned westward out into the open Atlantic.  The system was upgraded to a tropical depression on August 11th, but easterly vertical wind shear kept the system from strengthening, and led to a reduction of thunderstorm activity which devolved the system into a remnant low pressure area on the 13th.  By the 14th, the system redeveloped thunderstorm activity and redeveloped into a tropical depression, which strengthened into a tropical storm early on the 15th well to the east of the Lesser Antilles. Late on the 15th, a tropical storm watch was issued for the U.S. Virgin Islands, when the system was located about 910 miles east southeast of Saint Croix.  At 5 am on the 16th, the tropical storm watch was extended to Puerto Rico, when the system was located about 850 miles southeast of San Juan. Soon afterwards, vertical wind shear from the west began to displace thunderstorms east and northeast of the center, beginning Ana's weakening trend.  Also, the high pressure system steering Ana caused the system to accelerate westward. The weakening tropical storm Ana crossed the Lesser Antilles, and was downgraded to a tropical depression on the 16th at 5 pm, when it was located about 440 miles east southeast of Saint Croix. In spite of this, the tropical storm watch was kept in effect in case of any unexpected changes. Ana's local effects were mainly periods of heavy rain, gusty winds and cloud to ground lightning, and some much needed rain to Saint Croix. During the day on the 17th, aircraft reconnaissance determined that the system had dissipated southwest of Puerto Rico, as it no longer had a low-level circulation. At 5 pm on the 17th, the tropical storm watch was discontinued for the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint Croix airport ASOS sensor indicated a wind gust of 44 miles per hour from the southeast in association with tropical depression Ana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms with heavy rain and gusty winds affected the island of Saint Thomas during the early morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm affected the center of Saint Thomas from Drake's Passage to Mountain Top. Several power and street light poles downed, street lights damaged, trees and branches down, and a couple of small mudslides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms with heavy rain and gusty winds affected the island of Saint Thomas during the early morning of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm affected Saint Thomas with heavy rain and gusty winds. Some homes were flooded, and other areas lost power. Rainfall amounts of 1.20 inches fell during a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry and stable conditions persisted across the U.S. Virgin Islands with limited shower activity which provided favorable conditions for brush fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire burnt hundred of acres of thick vegetation near Ha'Penny beach on Saint Croix south shore. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level perturbation in association with an upper level trough east of the Lesser Antilles induced scattered showers isolated thunderstorms across the U.S. Virgin islands. Rainfall accumulation of around half an inch was recorded at the Saint Thomas airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional periods of heavy rain led to minor flooding of poorly drained areas on Saint Thomas.
